content.full_text:
REMCHO, JOHANSEN & PURCELL
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

220 MONTGOMERY STREET, SUITE 800
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94104
415/398-6230      FAX:415/398-7256

September 21, 1990

Sandra Taylor
Technical Assistance Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re:  Request for Written Confirmation of Advice

Dear Ms. Taylor:

     I am writing on behalf of Assemblyman Patrick
Johnston to request written confirmation pursuant to
Government Code section 83114(b) of advice you provided by
telephone today, as summarized below.  I would also
appreciate a phone call at your earliest convenience to give
oral confirmation, or to inform me if my summary does not
accurately reflect your advice in any way.

     My question concerned the relationship between the
special election cycle contribution limits in Government Code
section 85305 and the fiscal year contribution limits in
Government Code sections 85301 and 85303.  Mr. Johnston has a
controlled committee for re-election to his seat in the 26th
Assembly District at the general election to be held November
6, 1990.  Contributions to this committee are subject to the
fiscal year contribution limits in sections 85301 and 85303.
Mr. Johnston also has a controlled committee for election to
the vacant seat in the 5th Senate District in the special
election that has been called for November 6, 1990.  The
vacancy opened on September 4, 1990, and the "special
election cycle" for purposes of contributions to that
committee runs from that date through November 6, 1990.  (If
necessary, a special runoff election will be held at a date
in early 1991 that has not yet been fixed.)

     You advised that, during the Senate special
election cycle, a contributor may contribute up to the
applicable limit to each of the committees, without the
contributions being aggregated.  Thus, an individual who has
not, during the current fiscal year, contributed to any
candidate committee controlled by Mr. Johnston, may, during
the current special election cycle for the Senate seat,

Sandra Taylor
Technical Assistance Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
September 21, 1990
Page 2

lawfully contribute, and the committees may lawfully accept,
up to $1,000 to Mr. Johnston's Assembly committee under
section 85301(a) and up to $1,000 to his Senate special
election committee under section 85305. The same principles
would apply to permit a political committee to contribute
up to $2,500 to each committee, and a broad based political
committee to contribute up to $5,000 to each committee.

You also advised that an individual who had
contributed $1,000 to the Assembly committee during the
current fiscal year but before the Senate seat became vacant
could contribute up to $1,000 to the Senate special election
committee during the special election cycle. Again, the same
principle would apply to contributions at the higher limits
by a political committee or broad based political committee.

In the event that Mr. Johnston is a candidate in a
special runoff election for the Senate seat, a contributor
who has previously contributed to the Assembly committee and
to the Senate committee during the special election cycle may
lawfully make an additional contribution to the Senate
committee up to the applicable limit during the "special
runoff election cycle." The special runoff election cycle
begins the day after the special election and ends the day of
the special runoff election. For example, an individual who,
during the special election cycle, contributed $1,000 to Mr.
Johnston's Assembly committee and $1,000 to Mr. Johnston's
Senate committee, may contribute an additional $1,000 to the
Senate committee during the special runoff election cycle.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this request
for written confirmation of advice.

Sincerely,

Lowell Finley

LF/bk
